domain: Irish sports broadcasting and TV listings
key_findings: Ireland has 5 major sport categories dominating weekend TV: GAA (hurling & football), rugby (Six Nations, URC, Champions Cup), soccer (Premier League, League of Ireland, Champions League), horse racing (Cheltenham, Irish festivals), and golf (Ryder Cup, majors)
Weekend scheduling is the critical window — Saturday 12:30–22:00 and Sunday 13:00–18:00 are peak sports viewing times
GAA All-Ireland Championship matches (hurling & football) run May–September and are the most-watched domestic sporting events
Broadcast rights are fragmented across free-to-air (RTÉ, TG4, Virgin Media) and pay/streaming (Sky Sports, TNT Sports, Premier Sports, DAZN, NOW TV, GAA GO)
RTÉ holds free-to-air rights for major GAA championship matches and Six Nations rugby — these are protected 'listed events' under Irish law
TG4 broadcasts a significant number of GAA matches with Irish-language commentary
Premier League kick-off times are staggered: 12:30, 15:00 (blackout — not televised in UK/Ireland), 17:30 Saturday; 14:00, 16:30 Sunday
The 3pm Saturday blackout rule means no live Premier League games are shown between 14:45–17:15 on Saturdays in Ireland and UK
Horse racing fixtures are frequent (weekends year-round) and split between RTÉ, Racing TV, and Sky Sports Racing
Users expect times in Irish time (GMT in winter / IST i.e. GMT+1 in summer) — daylight saving transitions matter
market_context: No single dominant Irish sports TV guide exists. RTÉ Sport, the42.ie, and OffsideRule.com publish partial weekend schedules. Sky and Virgin Media list only their own channels. Most fans rely on social media, WhatsApp groups, or manually checking multiple broadcaster sites. A consolidated single-page weekend view is a genuine gap.
regulations: Broadcasting Act 2009 (Section 162): Designated 'events of major importance' must be available on free-to-air TV (e.g., All-Ireland finals, Six Nations home matches, FIFA World Cup)
The 'closed period' / 3pm blackout rule: No live coverage of football matches on Saturday between 14:45 and 17:15 (Article 48 of the UEFA/FA regulations, enforced in Ireland)
Copyright: Listing broadcast times and channel names is factual data and not subject to copyright, but rebroadcasting logos or streams would be
GDPR is not relevant as no user data is collected in a static listings tool

terminology.GAA: Gaelic Athletic Association — governs hurling, Gaelic football, camogie, and handball
terminology.Throw-in: The start of a GAA match (equivalent to 'kick-off' in soccer)
terminology.Puck-out: Restart in hurling after a score
terminology.TG4: Irish-language free-to-air TV channel, major GAA broadcaster
terminology.RT\u00c9: Raidió Teilifís Éireann — Ireland's national public broadcaster
terminology.URC: United Rugby Championship — cross-nation club rugby league featuring Irish provinces
terminology.Listed events: Sporting events legally required to be broadcast on free-to-air television
terminology.NOW TV: Sky's streaming-only service, common way Irish viewers access Sky Sports without a dish
terminology.GAA GO: GAA's own streaming platform for live and on-demand GAA matches (subscription)
terminology.Virgin Media Sport: Irish pay-TV sports channels (Champions League, La Liga rights)

key_players: RTÉ Sport (free-to-air: GAA, Six Nations, horse racing, Olympics)
TG4 (free-to-air: GAA matches in Irish language)
Sky Sports / NOW TV (Premier League, GAA, golf majors, F1)
Virgin Media Sport (Champions League, Europa League)
TNT Sports (formerly BT Sport — Champions League, URC rugby)
Premier Sports (URC, Serie A, Scottish football)
the42.ie (Irish sports media — publishes weekend TV schedules editorially)
researcher_notes: The tool name 'What time's Throw-in?' cleverly uses GAA terminology, appealing to Irish sports culture. Key build considerations: (1) Since there's no database, sport fixture data will need to be hardcoded or structured as a JSON blob within the HTML — consider making it easy to update weekly. (2) Time display must respect Irish time (GMT/IST) and ideally auto-detect user timezone. (3) The 3pm Saturday blackout is a common source of confusion — calling it out in the UI would add value. (4) Channel logos would enhance UX but may have trademark issues — use text labels instead. (5) Weekend-focused layout should default to 'this weekend' view with Saturday and Sunday as primary columns. (6) Mobile-first design is important — fans check on phones.
